questions.question: Explain the five OSPF packet types and their functions.
keyPoints: Hello - Used for neighbor discovery and maintaining neighbor relationships
DBD (Database Description) - Contains summary of LSDB, describes LSA headers
LSR (Link State Request) - Requests specific LSA information from neighbor
LSU (Link State Update) - Sends requested LSA information to neighbors
LSAck - Acknowledges receipt of OSPF packets

questions.question: Describe the different OSPF LSA types and their purposes.
keyPoints: Type 1 (Router LSA) - Generated by each router, describes router's links within area
Type 2 (Network LSA) - Generated by DR on broadcast networks, lists all routers on segment
Type 3 (Summary LSA) - Generated by ABR, describes routes to other areas
Type 4 (Summary LSA) - Generated by ABR, describes ASBR location to other areas
Type 5 (External LSA) - Generated by ASBR, describes external routes

questions.question: Explain OSPF neighbor states in sequence with brief description.
keyPoints: Down State - No Hello packets received
Init State - Hello received from neighbor but not bidirectionally
2-Way State - Bidirectional communication established, DR/BDR election occurs
ExStart State - Master/Slave relationship established for DBD exchange
Exchange State - Routers exchange DBD packets describing LSDB
Loading State - LSR/LSU exchange for requested LSAs
Full State - Complete adjacency, LSDB synchronized

questions.question: What is OSPF Stub Area and Totally Stub Area? Differentiate between them.
keyPoints: Stub Area - Does not allow Type 5 (external) LSAs, uses default route instead; reduces LSDB size
Totally Stub Area - Cisco proprietary; blocks Type 3, 4, and 5 LSAs; uses only default route for all external and inter-area routes
Both Stub types reduce routing table size and LSA flooding within area

questions.question: Explain the OSPF cost metric calculation with an example.
keyPoints: OSPF cost = Reference Bandwidth / Interface Bandwidth
Default reference bandwidth = 100 Mbps
Examples: 10 Mbps = cost 10, 100 Mbps = cost 1, 1 Gbps = cost 1, 100 Mbps loopback = cost 1
Cost can be manually modified using 'ip ospf cost' command
Lower cost indicates preferred path; equal costs enable load balancing

questions.question: What is the purpose of DR and BDR in OSPF? Explain the election process.
keyPoints: DR (Designated Router) - Reduces adjacency overhead on multi-access networks
BDR (Backup Designated Router) - Provides redundancy if DR fails
Election based on: Highest OSPF priority (0-255, default 1), then highest Router ID
All other routers become DROTHER and form full adjacency only with DR and BDR
Purpose: Reduces O(n²) adjacencies to O(n), minimizes LSA flooding

questions.question: Explain the Dijkstra's SPF algorithm operation in OSPF.
keyPoints: Algorithm runs on LSDB to calculate shortest path to each destination
Process: 1) Start with root router, 2) Add directly connected networks (cost 0)
3) Examine LSAs from neighbors, calculate cumulative cost to each destination
4) Add lowest-cost paths to Shortest Path Tree (SPT)
5) Iterate until all networks in area are in SPT
SPF recalculation triggered when LSA changes occur

questions.question: Describe the different types of OSPF areas and their characteristics. Include Stub, Totally Stub, and NSSA.
keyPoints: Backbone Area (Area 0) - Central area connecting all other areas; all inter-area traffic passes through it
Standard Area - Normal area that receives all LSA types (1-5)
Stub Area - Blocks Type 5 LSAs; replaces with default route; reduces LSDB size
Totally Stub Area (Cisco) - Blocks Type 3, 4, and 5 LSAs; only default route for external/inter-area
NSSA (Not So Stubby Area) - Allows external routes as Type 7 LSAs; ABR converts Type 7 to Type 5
Virtual Link - Connects area to Area 0 through non-backbone area when physical connection impossible

questions.question: Explain the OSPF adjacency formation process with all steps involved.
keyPoints: Step 1: Down State - Initial state, no information exchanged
Step 2: Init State - Router receives Hello, adds neighbor but doesn't confirm bidirectional
Step 3: 2-Way State - Bidirectional confirmed, DR/BDR election on multi-access networks
Step 4: ExStart - Master/Slave relationship determined (higher RID = master), DBD sequence number established
Step 5: Exchange - Routers exchange DBD packets containing LSA headers/summaries
Step 6: Loading - For unknown/more recent LSAs, send LSR and receive LSU with full LSA details
Step 7: Full State - LSDB synchronized, adjacency complete, ready for routing
Keepalive every 10 seconds (Hello) maintains adjacency

questions.question: Compare OSPF with RIP addressing key differences in routing algorithm, metrics, convergence, and scalability.
keyPoints: Algorithm: OSPF uses link-state (Dijkstra's SPF), RIP uses distance-vector (Bellman-Ford)
Metric: OSPF uses cost (bandwidth-based), RIP uses hop count (1-15)
Convergence: OSPF fast (triggered updates), RIP slow (periodic 30 sec updates)
Scalability: OSPF scales well with areas, RIP limited to 15 hops
VLSM: OSPF supports VLSM, RIP v1 does not
Update: OSPF partial triggered updates, RIP full periodic table updates
Hop Limit: OSPF unlimited, RIP maximum 15 hops
Administrative Distance: OSPF 110, RIP 120

questions.question: What is OSPF Router ID? How is it selected? Explain the election process and its importance.
keyPoints: Router ID (RID) - 32-bit unique identifier for each OSPF router
Selection Priority: 1) Manually configured RID (highest priority), 2) Highest loopback interface IP, 3) Highest active physical interface IP
Importance: Used for DR/BDR election, identifying routers in LSDB, advertising routes
Once selected, RID does not change unless router reloads or RID command reconfigured
Should be manually configured for stability and predictability in large networks
Used in all OSPF communications as router identifier
